topics.title: Computerized EEG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT754 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computerized EEG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Computerized EEG — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the electrical activity of the brain by placing multiple electrodes along the scalp. **Computerized EEG** refers to the modern digital implementation of EEG wherein the analog bioelectric signals acquired from the scalp are conditioned, sampled, digitized, processed, and displayed using computer-based systems, enabling advanced signal analysis such as spectral decomposition, topographic mapping, and automated feature extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBMT754):** *Computerized EEG* is the application of digital signal processing (DSP), statistical analysis, and computer visualization techniques to electroencephalographic signals for clinical diagnosis, research, and brain-computer interfacing.

### 1.1.1 Origin of the EEG Signal

The EEG signal originates from the **postsynaptic potentials** (specifically, the summed excitatory and inhibitory dendritic potentials) of cortical pyramidal neurons in the cerebral cortex. When large populations of these neurons fire synchronously, the resulting ionic currents generate measurable voltage fluctuations on the scalp surface.

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Signal Amplitude | **10 – 300 μV** |
| Signal Frequency Range | **0.1 – 100 Hz** |
| Clinical Bandwidth | **0.5 – 70 Hz** |
| Electrode Count (Standard) | **21 (10–20 system)** |
| Sampling Rate (Standard) | **256 – 512 Hz** |
| ADC Resolution | **12 – 24 bits** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Listening to a Stadium Crowd"**
> Imagine you are standing outside a massive football stadium and trying to understand what is happening inside. You cannot see the players, but you can hear a faint murmur. By placing **microphones at different positions** around the stadium, you record low-level sounds. A **computerized audio analyzer** then filters out the crowd noise, amplifies the relevant sounds, and runs a **spectrum analysis** to tell you the chant, the mood, and even the moment of a goal.
> 
> In the same way, the **scalp electrodes are microphones**, the **brain is the stadium**, and the **computer is the analyzer** that decodes the "neural chorus" into meaningful information.

## 1.3 Why "Computerized" EEG?

Traditional (paper-based) EEG used analog amplifiers and pen-and-ink recorders. Modern computerized EEG adds four major capabilities:

1. **Digital Storage** — Petabytes of EEG can be archived on hard drives/cloud.
2. **Signal Processing** — Filtering, artifact rejection, and spectral analysis via **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**.
3. **Visualization** — 2D/3D brain maps, spectral topograms, and time-frequency plots.
4. **Automation** — Automatic spike detection, sleep staging, seizure detection, and BCI control.

## 1.4 Clinical & Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Computerized EEG is the **backbone** of modern clinical neurophysiology. It is used in:
> - Epilepsy diagnosis and seizure localization
> - Sleep disorder studies (Polysomnography)
> - Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)
> - Anesthesia depth monitoring (BIS)
> - Cognitive neuroscience research
> - Neurofeedback and rehabilitation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Computerized EEG Acquisition Chain

The complete signal flow is structured as a **cascaded linear system** with the following stages:

1. **Electrodes (Transducers)** — Convert ionic current to electronic voltage.
2. **Differential Amplifier** — Rejects common-mode noise (CMRR > 100 dB).
3. **Analog Bandpass Filter** — Removes DC drift and high-frequency interference.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Samples the analog signal.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — Applies digital filters and FFT.
6. **Display & Storage** — Renders waveforms and stores data.

## 2.2 Electrode Placement — The 10–20 International System

The **10–20 system** is the standard electrode placement protocol defined by the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)**. Electrode positions are determined as **10%** and **20%** fractions of measured skull landmarks (nasion, inion, preauricular points).

### 2.2.1 Electrode Nomenclature

| Site | Letter Code |
|---|---|
| Frontal Pole | **Fp1, Fp2** |
| Frontal | **F3, F4, Fz, F7, F8** |
| Central | **C3, C4, Cz** |
| Temporal | **T3, T4, T5, T6** |
| Parietal | **P3, P4, Pz** |
| Occipital | **O1, O2** |
| Reference | **A1, A2 (earlobes)** |
| Ground | **Fpz** |

> Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7) = **left hemisphere**; Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8) = **right hemisphere**; 'z' = midline.

## 2.3 EEG Frequency Bands (Rhythms)

The clinical classification of EEG rhythms is based on **spectral power distribution** derived from the FFT.

| Rhythm | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (μV) | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 20 – 200 | Deep sleep, infants, pathology |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | < 20 | Drowsiness, meditation, memory |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | < 20 | Active thinking, alert, anxious |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | < 10 | Perception, cognition, binding |

## 2.4 The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To faithfully reconstruct an analog EEG signal of bandwidth **B Hz**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:
> 
> $$f_s \geq 2B$$
> 
> Since clinical EEG bandwidth is typically 0.5 – 70 Hz, the **minimum** sampling rate is **140 Hz**, but in practice **256 Hz or 512 Hz** is used to allow for anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

## 2.5 The Power Spectral Density (PSD)

The PSD describes how the signal power is distributed across frequency. For an EEG segment $x(t)$ of length $T$:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \left\vert X(f) \right\vert^{2}$$

where $X(f)$ is the Fourier Transform of $x(t)$. In practice, the **Welch method** is used: the signal is divided into overlapping windows, the FFT of each window is computed, and the squared magnitudes are averaged.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s = 2B$ | Hz |
| 2 | Spectral Power | $P = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{xx}(f) \, df$ | μV²/Hz |
| 3 | Band Power (e.g., Alpha) | $P_{\alpha} = \int_{8}^{13} S_{xx}(f) \, df$ | μV² |
| 4 | Relative Band Power | $P_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{band}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}} \right)$ | dB |
| 6 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$ | dB |
| 7 | Discrete Fourier Transform | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | — |
| 8 | DFT Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Hz |
| 9 | Electrode Skin Impedance (target) | $Z < 5 \text{ k}\Omega$ | kΩ |
| 10 | Montage (Bipolar) | $V_{\text{ch}} = V_{\text{electrode1}} - V_{\text{electrode2}}$ | μV |

## 2.7 Montages — How Channels Are Derived

A **montage** is the logical arrangement of electrode pairs to form display channels. Two principal types:

- **Referential (Monopolar)**: Each channel = Active Electrode − Reference (e.g., A1, A2, or Cz).
- **Bipolar**: Each channel = difference between two adjacent active electrodes.

## 2.8 Artifacts in Computerized EEG

| Artifact | Source | Frequency | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EOG** (Eye blink) | Corneo-retinal dipole | < 4 Hz | Regression, ICA |
| **EMG** (Muscle) | Scalp muscles | > 20 Hz | Low-pass filter |
| **ECG** (Cardiac) | Heart pulse | ~1.2 Hz | Template subtraction |
| **Powerline** | 50/60 Hz interference | 50/60 Hz | Notch filter |
| **Sweat** | Skin potentials | < 0.5 Hz | High-pass filter |
| **Electrode pop** | Poor contact | Broadband | Re-prepare electrode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Independent Component Analysis (ICA)** is the gold-standard computerized method for blind source separation of EEG artifacts. It decomposes the multichannel EEG into statistically independent components, allowing automated or manual rejection of artifactual sources.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) for EEG Spectral Analysis

The DFT converts a finite-duration discrete-time signal $x[n]$ of length $N$ into its frequency-domain representation $X[k]$.

### Step 1 — Start with the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform

The Fourier Transform of a continuous signal $x(t)$ is:

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f t} \, dt$$

**Reasoning:** The Fourier transform decomposes a time-domain signal into a sum of complex exponentials of different frequencies, each with a specific amplitude and phase.

### Step 2 — Sample the Signal at $f_s$

Replacing $t = n T_s$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$ and converting the integral to a discrete sum:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2 \pi f_k t_n}$$

where $f_k = k \cdot \Delta f$ and $t_n = n \cdot T_s$.

### Step 3 — Substitute to Get the DFT Formula

$$\boxed{X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}}$$

**Reasoning:** This summation computes the correlation between the signal and $N$ complex sinusoids at frequencies $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$.

### Step 4 — Frequency Resolution Derivation

The frequency spacing between adjacent DFT bins is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$$

**Reasoning:** Since there are $N$ samples spanning the total frequency range $0$ to $f_s/2$ (Nyquist), dividing $f_s/2$ by $N/2$ yields $f_s/N$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Frequency Resolution

**Problem:** A 2-second EEG epoch is sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz. What is the frequency resolution $\Delta f$?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the total number of samples:**

$$N = f_s \times T = 256 \times 2 = 512 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the resolution formula:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{256}{512} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Each DFT bin spans **0.5 Hz**. This means the alpha band (8–13 Hz) is resolved by approximately 10 bins, allowing clear separation of alpha peaks from theta noise.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample EEG Spectrum Interpretation

Suppose a 4-second EEG epoch from electrode **O1** (occipital) is analyzed. The relative band powers are:

| Band | Power (μV²) | Relative Power (%) | Clinical Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delta (0.5–4 Hz) | 4.2 | **10.5** | Normal |
| Theta (4–8 Hz) | 3.8 | **9.5** | Normal |
| Alpha (8–13 Hz) | 25.6 | **64.0** | **High** — eyes closed, relaxed |
| Beta (13–30 Hz) | 5.2 | **13.0** | Normal |
| Gamma (30–70 Hz) | 1.2 | **3.0** | Normal |
| **Total** | 40.0 | 100.0 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Dominant **alpha power (64%)** in the occipital region during an eyes-closed resting state is a textbook finding. This is known as the **"posterior dominant rhythm"** and confirms normal cortical function.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Inverse DFT (used for signal reconstruction)

Starting from $X[k]$, the time-domain signal is reconstructed by:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, e^{j 2 \pi k n / N}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the inverse of the forward DFT. The $1/N$ factor normalizes the result so that the original signal amplitude is recovered.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Welch Periodogram for PSD Estimation

### Step 1 — Segment the Signal

Divide $x[n]$ of length $N$ into $L$ overlapping segments of length $M$:

$$x^{(i)}[n] = x[n + iD], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$$

where $D$ is the hop size, often $D = M/2$ (50% overlap).

### Step 2 — Apply a Window Function

Multiply each segment by a window $w[n]$ (e.g., Hann or Hamming) to reduce spectral leakage:

$$x_w^{(i)}[n] = x^{(i)}[n] \cdot w[n]$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Periodogram of Each Segment

$$\hat{S}^{(i)}(f) = \frac{1}{M \cdot U} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x_w^{(i)}[n] \, e^{-j 2 \pi f n} \right\vert^{2}$$

where $U = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w^2[n]$ is the window energy normalization factor.

### Step 4 — Average the Periodograms

$$\boxed{\hat{S}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} \hat{S}^{(i)}(f)}$$

**Reasoning:** Averaging $L$ periodograms reduces the variance of the PSD estimate by a factor of $L$, at the cost of reduced frequency resolution.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Welch PSD Computation Parameters

**Problem:** A 10-second EEG epoch sampled at $f_s = 512$ Hz is to be analyzed using Welch's method with $M = 1024$ samples per segment and 50% overlap. Compute (a) segment duration, (b) hop size, and (c) number of segments $L$.

### Solution:

**(a) Segment duration:**

$$T_{\text{seg}} = \frac{M}{f_s} = \frac{1024}{512} = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

**(b) Hop size:**

$$D = \frac{M}{2} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512 \text{ samples}$$

In time: $D/f_s = 512/512 = 1.0$ s shift per segment.

**(c) Number of segments:**

$$L = \frac{N - M}{D} + 1 = \frac{5120 - 1024}{512} + 1 = \frac{4096}{512} + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ segments}$$

This gives a **9-fold variance reduction** in the PSD estimate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computerized EEG — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of a computerized EEG system, from scalp acquisition to clinical display.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Scalp and Cortical Source] --> A1[Electrodes Ag or AgCl 10 to 20 system]
    A1 --> A2[Electrode Skin Interface Conductive gel Impedance check]
    A2 --> A3[Preamplifier Instrumentation amp High CMRR]
    A3 --> A4[Analog Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 70 Hz]
    A4 --> A5[Notch Filter 50 or 60 Hz rejection]
    A5 --> A6[Analog to Digital Converter 16 to 24 bit 256 to 1024 Hz]
    A6 --> A7[Digital Signal Processor DSP unit]
    A7 --> A8[Digital Filters FIR or IIR]
    A8 --> A9[Artifact Removal ICA or Regression]
    A9 --> A10[Spectral Analysis FFT or Welch PSD]
    A10 --> A11[Feature Extraction Band power Coherence]
    A11 --> A12[Classification Seizure detection Sleep staging]
    A12 --> A13[Display Monitor Topographic map Spectrogram]
    A13 --> A14[Database Storage DICOM or EDF format]
    A14 --> A15[Clinician Review and Report]
```

## 4.2 EEG Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Raw EEG Multichannel] --> S2[Preprocessing Re referencing]
    S2 --> S3[Filtering Band pass 0.5 to 70 Hz]
    S3 --> S4[Segmentation Epochs 2 to 4 s]
    S4 --> S5[Artifact Detection ICA or Threshold]
    S5 --> S6[Clean EEG]
    S6 --> S7[Time Domain Analysis Event related potentials]
    S6 --> S8[Frequency Domain FFT or PSD]
    S6 --> S9[Time Frequency Wavelet or STFT]
    S7 --> S10[Visualization]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Clinical Report]
```

## 4.3 Frequency Band Selection and Clinical Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[EEG Signal] --> F1{Band Selection}
    F1 --> F2[Delta 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    F1 --> F3[Theta 4 to 8 Hz]
    F1 --> F4[Alpha 8 to 13 Hz]
    F1 --> F5[Beta 13 to 30 Hz]
    F1 --> F6[Gamma above 30 Hz]
    F2 --> C2[Deep Sleep or Pathological]
    F3 --> C3[Drowsiness or Memory Encoding]
    F4 --> C4[Relaxed Wakefulness Eyes Closed]
    F5 --> C5[Active Thinking or Alertness]
    F6 --> C6[Cognition or Sensory Binding]
```

## 4.4 Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) Loop Using EEG

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U0[User Imagines Movement] --> U1[Cortical Mu Rhythm 8 to 12 Hz]
    U1 --> U2[EEG Electrodes over Motor Cortex]
    U2 --> U3[Computerized Amplifier and ADC]
    U3 --> U4[Feature Extraction Band Power]
    U4 --> U5[Classifier LDA or SVM]
    U5 --> U6{Decision}
    U6 --> U7[Move Cursor Right]
    U6 --> U8[Move Cursor Left]
    U6 --> U9[Select Item]
    U7 --> U10[Feedback to User Visual or Haptic]
    U8 --> U10
    U9 --> U10
    U10 --> U0
```

## 4.5 Computerized EEG vs. Conventional EEG — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Conventional EEG] --> P2[Analog Amplifier]
    P2 --> P3[Pen and Paper Recorder]
    P3 --> P4[Manual Visual Review]
    P4 --> P5[Limited Storage]
    P5 --> P6[No Quantitative Analysis]

    Q1[Computerized EEG] --> Q2[Analog Front End]
    Q2 --> Q3[High Resolution ADC]
    Q3 --> Q4[Digital Filtering and ICA]
    Q4 --> Q5[Spectral and Time Frequency Analysis]
    Q5 --> Q6[Automated Detection and Mapping]
    Q6 --> Q7[Cloud Database and AI Report]
    Q7 --> Q8[Quantitative Metrics and Trends]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Computerized EEG. List any four advantages of computerized EEG over conventional analog EEG.*

**Model Answer:**

**Computerized EEG** is the digital acquisition, processing, analysis, and storage of brain electrical signals using computer-based systems equipped with analog-to-digital converters, digital signal processors, and visualization software.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Digital Storage** — Large volumes of data can be stored compactly on servers/cloud for long-term review.
2. **Advanced Signal Processing** — Enables digital filtering, FFT, ICA, and wavelet analysis.
3. **Automated Detection** — Algorithms can automatically detect spikes, seizures, and sleep stages.
4. **Reproducible Quantitative Analysis** — Provides numerical metrics (band power, coherence) for objective clinical comparison.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the 10–20 international system of electrode placement in EEG. Why is it called "10–20"?*

**Model Answer:**

The **10–20 system** is the internationally standardized method for placing scalp electrodes. It uses the skull landmarks **nasion (Nz)**, **inion (Iz)**, and **left/right preauricular points (A1, A2)** as reference.

The skull circumference (nasion to inion via the top) is divided at intervals of **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%**, and similarly for the transverse (left preauricular to right preauricular) axis. Hence the name **"10–20"** reflects the 10% and 20% fractional divisions. It typically yields **21 electrodes** including reference and ground.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern**, Part B questions offer an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Module 2]** *(a) Describe the block diagram of a computerized EEG system with the function of each block. (7 marks)*  
*(b) Explain the EEG frequency bands (Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma) with their frequency ranges, typical amplitudes, and associated physiological states. (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

A computerized EEG system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Electrodes (Transducers)** — Ag/AgCl electrodes placed on the scalp convert ionic currents into electronic voltage. Skin impedance is kept below **5 kΩ** using conductive gel. **(1 mark)**

2. **Differential Preamplifier** — A high-CMRR (>100 dB) instrumentation amplifier boosts the microvolt-level EEG signal while rejecting common-mode interference. **(1 mark)**

3. **Analog Bandpass Filter** — Limits the signal bandwidth to **0.5–70 Hz** to remove DC drift and high-frequency noise. **(1 mark)**

4. **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz)** — Rejects powerline interference. **(0.5 mark)**

5. **ADC** — Samples the analog signal at $f_s = 256$–$512$ Hz with 12–24 bit resolution. **(1 mark)**

6. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — Applies digital filters (FIR/IIR), runs FFT, performs artifact removal using ICA. **(1 mark)**

7. **Display Unit** — Renders multi-channel waveforms, topograms, and spectrograms. **(0.5 mark)**

8. **Storage** — Archives raw and processed data in **EDF** (European Data Format) or **DICOM** format. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

| Rhythm | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (μV) | State | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 20 – 200 | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants, severe organic brain disease | 1.5 |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | < 20 | Drowsiness, light sleep, emotional stress, memory encoding | 1.5 |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, posterior dominant rhythm | 1.5 |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | < 20 | Active mental activity, alertness, anxious states | 1.0 |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | < 10 | Higher cognitive processing, sensory binding, perception | 1.0 |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The five rhythms form a hierarchical spectral signature of cortical activity, and computerized EEG enables precise quantification of each band's power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 — Module 2]** *(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the signal processing pipeline of computerized EEG from raw acquisition to clinical interpretation. (7 marks)*  
*(b) Derive the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) formula used for EEG spectral analysis. Compute the frequency resolution for a 4-second epoch sampled at 256 Hz. (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The computerized EEG signal processing pipeline consists of:

1. **Acquisition** — Multichannel EEG (typically 19–32 channels) acquired at 256–512 Hz. **(1 mark)**
2. **Re-referencing** — Common Average Reference (CAR) or linked-ears reference. **(1 mark)**
3. **Filtering** — Bandpass (0.5–70 Hz) and notch (50/60 Hz). **(1 mark)**
4. **Artifact Removal** — Independent Component Analysis (ICA) to remove EOG, EMG, ECG. **(1 mark)**
5. **Segmentation** — Epochs of 2–4 s for analysis. **(1 mark)**
6. **Spectral/Time-Frequency Analysis** — Welch PSD, Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), Wavelet. **(1 mark)**
7. **Feature Extraction & Classification** — Band power, coherence, machine learning for seizure detection. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform:

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f t} \, dt$$

Sampling $x(t)$ at $t = nT_s$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}$$

**Reasoning:** This sum represents the correlation between the signal and $N$ complex sinusoids at frequencies $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$. The exponential $e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}$ rotates a unit phasor, and summing these weighted by the signal samples yields the spectral coefficient. **(2 marks for steps shown)**

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given: $T = 4$ s, $f_s = 256$ Hz

$$N = f_s \times T = 256 \times 4 = 1024 \text{ samples}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Interpretation:** Each DFT bin spans 0.25 Hz, allowing fine discrimination of sub-bands within alpha and theta ranges.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing ADC resolution with sampling rate** — Resolution (bits) is about amplitude quantization; sampling rate is about time discretization. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units in frequency resolution** — Always write **Hz** explicitly. A bare numerical answer loses **0.5 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up montage types** — Referential ≠ Bipolar. Clearly state the reference electrode used.
> 
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in Part B** — KTU examiners award partial credit for each algebraic transition. Skipping directly to the final formula forfeits **up to 3 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning CMRR** — A digital EEG system is only as good as its analog front end. Omitting CMRR specification in the block diagram description loses **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Computerized EEG**

- **Definition:** Computerized EEG = digital acquisition + DSP + automated analysis of brain electrical signals.
- **Signal Origin:** Summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons.
- **Amplitude:** **10–300 μV**; **Bandwidth:** **0.5–70 Hz**.
- **10–20 System:** 21 electrodes; odd = left, even = right, 'z' = midline.
- **Five Rhythms:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30 Hz).
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2B$; standard sampling at 256 or 512 Hz.
- **DFT:** $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}$
- **Frequency Resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / N$
- **PSD Method:** Welch's averaged periodogram with 50% overlap.
- **CMRR:** > 100 dB; **Electrode Impedance:** < 5 kΩ.
- **Artifacts:** EOG, EMG, ECG, 50/60 Hz, sweat — removed by ICA.
- **Montages:** Referential (monopolar) vs. Bipolar.
- **File Formats:** EDF (European Data Format), DICOM.
- **Applications:** Epilepsy, sleep studies, BCI, anesthesia depth, neurofeedback.
- **Clinical Signature:** Posterior dominant alpha rhythm with eyes closed.
- **Block Diagram Order:** Electrode → Amp → Filter → Notch → ADC → DSP → Display → Storage.
- **ICA** = Independent Component Analysis; gold standard for artifact removal.
- **PSD units:** μV²/Hz; **Band Power units:** μV².
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ECG and EMG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT754 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ECG and EMG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive bioelectric recording of the electrical potential generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle cells over time, captured via surface electrodes placed on the body. It is the gold-standard diagnostic modality for evaluating cardiac rhythm, conduction abnormalities, ischemia, infarction, and structural pathologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ECG measures the **summed vector potential** of the heart's electrical dipole, NOT individual cell action potentials. The standard clinical amplitude is **1 mV**, and the diagnostic bandwidth is **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine the heart as a large battery slowly moving inside your chest. Every time it "fires" (depolarization), a tiny electrical wave ripples across the body. The ECG is like placing microphones (electrodes) at different corners of the room to listen to that wave from various angles. By comparing the signals at different microphone positions, doctors can reconstruct *where* and *when* the wave moved — much like triangulating a sound source.

---

### 1.2 Electromyogram (EMG)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
The **Electromyogram (EMG)** is a biosignal that records the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscle fibers during voluntary or involuntary contraction, derived from either surface electrodes (sEMG) or invasive needle electrodes (nEMG). It is used clinically for diagnosing neuromuscular disorders and in engineering applications such as **prosthetic control**, **rehabilitation robotics**, and **human–computer interfaces**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** ECG originates from *cardiac* muscle (autonomic, synchronized, rhythmic). EMG originates from *skeletal* muscle (somatic, on-demand, spatially distributed). Their acquisition hardware is similar but the signal processing differs significantly.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of EMG as a "loudness meter" for your muscles. When your bicep is relaxed, the meter reads silence (baseline noise). As you start curling a dumbbell, the meter spikes. The harder and more muscles you recruit, the louder the signal. Surface EMG is like a microphone placed on the outside of a concert hall; needle EMG is like putting a microphone *inside* one specific instrument.

---

### 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | ECG | EMG |
|---|---|---|
| **Source organ** | Heart (cardiac muscle) | Skeletal muscle |
| **Amplitude** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **50 µV – 5 mV** |
| **Frequency range** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | **20 – 500 Hz** |
| **Default sampling rate** | **250 – 500 Hz** | **1000 – 2000 Hz** |
| **Diagnostic use** | Cardiac rhythm, MI | Neuropathy, myopathy |
| **Engineering use** | HR monitoring, fitness | Prosthetics, HCI |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ECG — The Cardiac Dipole and Einthoven's Triangle

The heart functions as an **equivalent electrical dipole** whose vector $\vec{M}$ changes magnitude and orientation throughout the cardiac cycle. Surface electrodes detect the *projection* of this dipole onto the lead axis.

**Einthoven's Triangle (Bipolar Limb Leads):**
Three electrodes — Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), Left Leg (LL) — form an equilateral triangle around the heart. The standard bipolar leads are defined as potential differences:

$$\text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$\text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$\text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Einthoven's Law (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied):**

$$\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = \text{Lead II}$$

**Augmented Unipolar Leads (Goldberger):**

$$aV_R = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

$$aV_L = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

$$aV_F = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}$$

The **12-lead ECG** = 3 bipolar (I, II, III) + 3 augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF) + 6 precordial (V1–V6).

---

### 2.2 ECG Waveform Components (The PQRST Complex)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU question on ECG requires drawing and labelling the **P-QRS-T complex**. Memorise the sequence, amplitude, and timing.

| Wave | Physiological Event | Typical Duration | Typical Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | **80 – 110 ms** | **0.25 mV** |
| **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay | **120 – 200 ms** | — |
| **Q wave** | Initial septal depolarization | < 40 ms | < 0.25 mV (negative) |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | **80 – 120 ms** | **1.0 – 1.5 mV** |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (repolarization latent) | **80 – 120 ms** | Isoelectric |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | **160 ms** | **0.5 mV** |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity | **350 – 440 ms** | — |

---

### 2.3 EMG — The Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)

A **Motor Unit (MU)** = one motor neuron + all the muscle fibers it innervates. The electrical sum of all individual fiber action potentials within the territory of one MU is the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

**Signal Decomposition Hierarchy:**

$$\text{EMG signal} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \text{MUAP}_k(t) + n(t)$$

Where $N$ is the number of active motor units and $n(t)$ is additive noise (line interference, motion artefact, thermal noise).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations for ECG and EMG**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{ECG}(t) = \vec{M}(t) \cdot \hat{a}$ | Projection of cardiac dipole onto lead axis $\hat{a}$ | mV |
| 2 | $I + III = II$ | Einthoven's law (signal consistency check) | mV |
| 3 | $HR = \frac{60}{RR \text{ interval (s)}}$ | Instantaneous heart rate | bpm |
| 4 | $f_{c,ECG} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | ECG high-pass cutoff (typically 0.05 Hz) | Hz |
| 5 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio for amplifier design | dB |
| 6 | $f_{s} \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Minimum sampling rate: ECG ≥ 250 Hz, EMG ≥ 1000 Hz | Hz |
| 7 | $V_{EMG} = \sum_{i} A_i \cdot \phi_i(t - t_i)$ | EMG = sum of time-shifted MUAP wavelets | mV |
| 8 | $\text{RMS}_{EMG} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x_n^2}$ | Muscle activation level indicator | mV |
| 9 | $f_{mean} = \frac{\int f \cdot P(f)\,df}{\int P(f)\,df}$ | Mean power frequency (fatigue index) | Hz |
| 10 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10} \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}$ | Common-mode rejection ratio (≥ 80 dB for biopotential amps) | dB |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** ECG drives wearable fitness bands, Holter monitors, and ICU telemetry. EMG drives myoelectric prosthetic arms (e.g., DEKA LUKE Arm), exoskeletons, and gesture-recognition HCI systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from RR Interval

**Problem:** A patient's ECG paper recording shows two consecutive R-peaks separated by **20 small boxes** on standard ECG paper (where each small box = **1 mm** and paper speed = **25 mm/s**).

**Step 1 — Identify the time per small box.**

$$\text{Time per box} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the RR interval in seconds.**

$$RR = 20 \text{ boxes} \times 0.04 \text{ s/box} = 0.80 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the heart-rate formula.**

$$HR = \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{RR \text{ (s)}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$HR = \frac{60}{0.80} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$

> **Engineering insight:** The same principle applies to PPG-based smartwatches — they detect R-R (or pulse-to-pulse) intervals optically and compute HR in real time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling for ECG and EMG

**Problem:** Determine the minimum sampling rate for clinical ECG and EMG acquisition.

**Step 1 — State the Nyquist–Shannon theorem.**

A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed if and only if:

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to ECG.** Diagnostic ECG bandwidth extends to **150 Hz**.

$$f_{s,ECG} \ge 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

In practice, KTU clinical practice adopts **500 Hz** (4× oversampling) to preserve QRS morphology during ST-segment analysis.

**Step 3 — Apply to EMG.** Surface EMG extends to **500 Hz**.

$$f_{s,EMG} \ge 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

In practice, **2000 Hz** is the KTU-recommended rate for surface EMG to avoid aliasing of sharp MUAP transitions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

**Problem:** A biopotential amplifier has differential gain $A_d = 10{,}000$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.1$. Find the CMRR.

**Step 1 — Write the standard definition.**

$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10{,}000}{0.1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the ratio.**

$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 20 \log_{10}(10^5)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate using $\log_{10}(10^n) = n$.**

$$CMRR = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ dB}$$

> **Clinical relevance:** KTU/MPT 754 requires CMRR ≥ **80 dB** at 50/60 Hz to reject mains interference; **100 dB** is excellent for surgical / ICU use.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ECG vs. EMG Acquisition Parameters

| Parameter | ECG | sEMG | nEMG |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrode type** | Ag/AgCl wet gel | Ag/AgCl wet or dry | Concentric needle |
| **Standard placement** | 10-lead (12-lead) | Over muscle belly | Into muscle belly |
| **Bandwidth** | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 20 – 500 Hz | 20 – 5000 Hz |
| **Gain needed** | ×1000 | ×1000 | ×100 |
| **Sampling rate** | 500 Hz | 2000 Hz | 20 kHz |
| **CMRR req.** | ≥ 80 dB | ≥ 80 dB | ≥ 60 dB |
| **Typical artefact** | Motion, baseline wander | Crosstalk, sweat | Insertion activity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECG Acquisition Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heart Dipole Source] --> B[Surface Electrodes RA LA LL]
    B --> C[Patient Cable]
    C --> D[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    D --> E[0.05 Hz High-Pass Filter]
    E --> F[150 Hz Low-Pass Filter]
    F --> G[50/60 Hz Notch Filter]
    G --> H[16-bit ADC]
    H --> I[Microcontroller / DSP]
    I --> J[Display and Storage]
    J --> K[Clinician / Algorithm]
    K --> L[Diagnosis HR Arrhythmia MI]
```

### 4.2 ECG Waveform and Interval Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline] --> B[P Wave Atrial Depol]
    B --> C[PR Segment AV Node Delay]
    C --> D[Q Septal Depol]
    D --> E[R Main Vent Depol]
    E --> F[S Late Vent Depol]
    F --> G[ST Segment Plateau]
    G --> H[T Wave Vent Repol]
    H --> I[U Wave Optional]
    I --> J[Baseline Reset]
```

### 4.3 EMG Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Skeletal Muscle] --> B[sEMG Electrodes]
    B --> C[Differential Pre-Amp INA128]
    C --> D[20 Hz HPF Motion Artefact Removal]
    D --> E[500 Hz LPF Anti-Alias]
    E --> F[Notch 50/60 Hz]
    F --> G[Rectification Absolute Value]
    G --> H[Smoothing RMS or Envelope]
    H --> I[Feature Extraction MAV RMS ZC]
    I --> J[Classifier SVM LDA ANN]
    J --> K[Output Gesture or Force]
```

### 4.4 Einthoven's Triangle — Lead Formation Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RA[RA Right Arm] --- LA[LA Left Arm]
    LA --- LL[LL Left Leg]
    LL --- RA
    RA -->|Lead I minus| LI[Lead I LA minus RA]
    LA -->|Lead III minus| LIII[Lead III LL minus LA]
    RA -->|Lead II minus| LII[Lead II LL minus RA]
    LI --- ECGRec[ECG Recorder]
    LIII --- ECGRec
    LII --- ECGRec
```

### 4.5 ECG Electrode Placement — 12-Lead System Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LimbLeads[Limb Leads I II III aVR aVL aVF] --> Junction[Wilson Central Terminal]
    Junction --> Pre[Precordial Leads V1 V2 V3 V4 V5 V6]
    Pre --> Out[12-Lead ECG Output]
    LimbLeads --> Out
    Out --> Doc[Cardiologist Interpretation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** KTU 2024 question papers often include 2-mark "draw & label" or 5-mark "block diagram" sub-questions. Mermaid renders cleanly inside modern markdown viewers and is fully accessible for screen readers — preferred over raster scans.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. List the components of the PQRST complex and state the physiological event each represents.**

**Model Answer:**
The PQRST complex of a normal ECG consists of:
- **P wave** → Atrial depolarization.
- **QRS complex (Q, R, S)** → Ventricular depolarization.
- **T wave** → Ventricular repolarization.
- (Optional: **U wave** → Purkinje fiber repolarization, often invisible.)
Intervening intervals — **PR interval (120–200 ms)** reflects AV nodal conduction delay; **ST segment** is isoelectric and represents the ventricular plateau phase; **QT interval (350–440 ms)** represents total ventricular electrical activity.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between surface EMG and needle EMG in terms of invasiveness, signal bandwidth, and one clinical use.**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | sEMG (Surface) | nEMG (Needle) |
|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (percutaneous) |
| **Bandwidth** | 20 – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz |
| **Pickup volume** | Large (multiple MUs) | Small (few MUs) |
| **Clinical use** | Gait analysis, biofeedback, prosthetic control | Diagnosis of myopathy vs. neuropathy |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern: Module 2 Choice Question]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw and label the typical ECG waveform. Explain the origin and clinical significance of the P wave, QRS complex, T wave, and PR interval. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the ECG acquisition system. Justify the choice of the standard gain (×1000), bandwidth (0.05–150 Hz), and CMRR (≥ 80 dB) for clinical ECG amplifiers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Sketch** (student draws on answer sheet; key elements must be present and labelled):
- Vertical axis: voltage (**mV**), calibrated at **10 mm/mV**.
- Horizontal axis: time (**s**), at paper speed **25 mm/s**.
- Sequence: baseline → small upward **P wave** → flat **PR segment** → sharp **QRS complex** (small Q, tall R, deep S) → isoelectric **ST segment** → rounded **T wave** → baseline.

**Step 2 — Origin & clinical significance:**

| Wave / Interval | Origin | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial myocyte depolarization (sino-atrial node initiated) | Absent = atrial fibrillation; peaked = atrial enlargement; inverted in lead II = ectopic atrial rhythm |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | Widened (> 120 ms) = bundle branch block; tall R = ventricular hypertrophy; pathological Q = myocardial infarction |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | Inverted = ischemia; tall peaked = hyperkalemia; flattened = hypokalemia |
| **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay | Prolonged (> 200 ms) = first-degree AV block; short = WPW syndrome |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 mermaid block — student should redraw on answer script).

**Step 2 — Justifications:**

- **Gain ×1000:** Raw ECG amplitude is ~1 mV. ADC typically needs 0 – 5 V input. Therefore:
  $$\text{Required gain} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ mV}} = 5000 \text{ (peak)}; 1000 \text{ is sufficient for typical 1–5 mV signals.}$$
- **Bandwidth 0.05 – 150 Hz:**
  - High-pass at **0.05 Hz** removes DC offset and baseline wander due to respiration (0.1 – 0.5 Hz) without distorting the ST segment.
  - Low-pass at **150 Hz** preserves QRS notching while rejecting EMG crosstalk and 50/60 Hz mains noise.
- **CMRR ≥ 80 dB:** Mains interference (50/60 Hz) is common-mode to both inputs of a differential amplifier. CMRR of 80 dB attenuates 50 Hz noise by a factor of $10^4$, leaving an acceptable residual:
  $$\text{Noise rejection} = 10^{\frac{80}{20}} = 10{,}000 \times$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **PR interval** (start of P to start of QRS) with the **PR segment** (end of P to start of QRS). Examiners strictly deduct marks for this interchange. Also, do NOT state the gain as "1000 mV/V" — that is dimensionally wrong; the correct unit is **V/V** (unitless ratio).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define a Motor Unit. With supporting equations, explain the hierarchical model of an EMG signal. Discuss the clinical and engineering applications of EMG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast ECG and EMG with respect to source, amplitude range, bandwidth, sampling rate, and electrode type. Justify why two different sampling rates are mandated in clinical practice. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Motor Unit:**
A **Motor Unit (MU)** is the functional unit of neuromuscular control, comprising **one alpha motor neuron** in the ventral horn of the spinal cord, **its axon**, and **all the muscle fibers it innervates**. The number of fibers per MU ranges from 3–5 (extraocular muscles, fine control) to 1000+ (quadriceps, gross force).

**Step 2 — Hierarchical model equation:**

$$\text{EMG}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \text{MUAP}_k(t) + n(t)$$

Each MUAP itself is:

$$\text{MUAP}_k(t) = A_k \, \phi(t - t_k) + \text{nonlinear distortion terms}$$

- $A_k$ = recruitment weight (fiber density × proximity).
- $\phi(t)$ = template MUAP waveform.
- $t_k$ = firing instant.
- $n(t)$ = noise (line, thermal, motion).

**Step 3 — Applications:**

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Clinical** | Diagnose myopathy, neuropathy, ALS, myasthenia gravis |
| **Rehabilitation** | Biofeedback, gait analysis, stroke recovery monitoring |
| **Prosthetics** | Myoelectric hand/arm control (e.g., Ottobock bebionic) |
| **Sports science** | Muscle fatigue analysis via median frequency decline |
| **HCI** | Gesture recognition, silent speech interfaces |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | ECG | EMG (sEMG) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Cardiac muscle (autorhythmic) | Skeletal muscle (voluntary) |
| **Amplitude** | 0.5 – 5 mV | 50 µV – 5 mV |
| **Bandwidth** | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 20 – 500 Hz |
| **Sampling rate** | 250 – 500 Hz | 1000 – 2000 Hz |
| **Electrode** | Ag/AgCl wet gel, large plate | Smaller bipolar, often differential pair |

**Step 2 — Justification of different sampling rates:**
- **Nyquist requirement:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. ECG $f_{max}=150$ Hz → $f_s \ge 300$ Hz; EMG $f_{max}=500$ Hz → $f_s \ge 1000$ Hz.
- **Clinical safety margin:** Real systems sample at **3–5×** the Nyquist rate to:
  1. Tolerate anti-alias filter roll-off imperfections.
  2. Preserve sharp QRS notching / MUAP spikes.
  3. Allow digital down-sampling for display/feature extraction.

Therefore the standard 500 Hz (ECG) vs 2000 Hz (EMG) reflects a balance between **diagnostic fidelity** and **storage / power budget** in wearable devices.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistakes in this question:**
> 1. Writing the ECG sampling rate as "1 kHz" — examiners do deduct, because that is the EMG rate, not the ECG rate.
> 2. Confusing **sEMG** with **ECG** electrode types — they look similar but ECG uses larger plates with conductive gel to capture whole-heart vectors.
> 3. Forgetting to convert µV to mV when comparing amplitudes. Use **standard SI prefixes** consistently.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read 2 hours before exam):**

- **ECG** = electrical recording of heart; **EMG** = electrical recording of skeletal muscle.
- **Einthoven's Law:** $\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = \text{Lead II}$ — must hold for every valid ECG.
- **Standard calibration:** $10 \text{ mm/mV}$ amplitude, $25 \text{ mm/s}$ paper speed.
- **P wave** (atrial depol) → **PR interval** (AV delay) → **QRS** (vent depol) → **ST** (plateau) → **T wave** (vent repol).
- **Normal durations:** PR 120–200 ms · QRS 80–120 ms · QT 350–440 ms.
- **Heart rate formula:** $HR = 60 / RR$ where $RR$ is in seconds.
- **12-lead ECG** = 3 limb bipolar + 3 augmented + 6 precordial.
- **EMG signal model:** superposition of MUAPs + noise.
- **EMG features:** MAV, RMS, ZC, mean/median frequency.
- **Sampling theorem:** ECG ≥ 500 Hz, EMG ≥ 2000 Hz, both must exceed 2 × highest frequency component.
- **CMRR** of biopotential amplifier should be ≥ **80 dB** (clinical) and ideally **100 dB** (ICU).
- **Motor Unit** = 1 motor neuron + all fibers it innervates.
- **sEMG** is non-invasive and used in engineering; **nEMG** is invasive and used in clinical diagnosis of neuromuscular disease.
- **Real-world applications:** ECG → Holter, ICD, fitness bands. EMG → myoelectric prosthetics, exoskeletons, gesture HCI.
- **Engineering traps:** never confuse PR *interval* with PR *segment*; never write gain in mm/mV; always state units with every numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Assisted Medical Imaging - X-Ray
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT754 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Assisted Medical Imaging - X-Ray

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — COMPUTER ASSISTED MEDICAL IMAGING: X-RAY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer-Assisted Medical Imaging using X-Rays** is a non-invasive diagnostic modality that utilizes **ionizing electromagnetic radiation** (wavelength range **0.01 nm to 10 nm**, frequencies **30 PHz to 30 EHz**) to produce two-dimensional projection images (radiographs) or cross-sectional volumetric reconstructions (Computed Tomography / CT) of internal anatomical structures based on differential **tissue attenuation coefficients**.

Discovered accidentally by **Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen** on **8 November 1895**, X-ray imaging remains the **first**, **fastest**, and **most widely deployed** digital imaging modality in modern healthcare. Modern systems integrate **digital radiography (DR)**, **computed radiography (CR)**, **fluoroscopy**, **angiography**, and **multi-detector Computed Tomography (MDCT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard X-ray energy for diagnostic radiography:** **50 kVp to 150 kVp** (peak kilovoltage).
> **CT slice energy range:** **80 kVp to 140 kVp**.
> **Mean glandular dose (MGD) limit per mammogram:** **< 2.5 mGy**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shadow Puppet Principle"

Imagine holding a **flashlight (torch)** behind your hand in a dark room. Your bones cast a **darker shadow** than your soft tissue because they block more light. X-ray imaging works on the **exact same shadow principle**, except the "light" is an invisible, highly penetrating electromagnetic beam emitted by a vacuum tube.

| Everyday Object | X-Ray Equivalent | Underlying Physics |
|---|---|---|
| Flashlight beam | X-ray photon flux | Electromagnetic emission |
| Bones in hand | High-Z (atomic number) tissue | Strong photoelectric absorption |
| Skin/flesh | Low-Z soft tissue | Weak Compton scattering |
| Shadow on wall | Radiographic image on detector | 2D attenuation map |

The **computer-assisted** enhancement comes in *after* the image is acquired — through **Digital Image Processing (DIP)**, **windowing**, **edge enhancement**, **noise reduction (wavelet/BM3D)**, and **3D reconstruction via Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** or **iterative reconstruction (IR/ASIR/SAFIRE)**.

---

### 1.3 KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome Alignment

| CO Code | Course Outcome Statement | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Illustrate the principles of medical imaging modalities including X-ray, CT, MRI, and ultrasound. | Understand (L2) |
| **CO3** | Apply digital signal and image processing techniques to enhance diagnostic image quality. | Apply (L3) |
| **CO4** | Analyze the physical, technical, and safety parameters of imaging systems. | Analyze (L4) |

This topic directly serves **CO2 and CO4** of the official PEBMT754 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of X-Ray Production

X-rays are generated inside a **Coolidge-type vacuum diode tube** through two competing quantum mechanisms when high-velocity electrons strike a **tungsten (W, Z = 74)** or **molybdenum (Mo, Z = 42)** target anode.

### 2.1.1 Bremsstrahlung ("Braking Radiation") — ~70–80% of clinical X-rays

When a high-speed electron is **decelerated** by the strong Coulomb field of a target nucleus, it loses kinetic energy and emits a continuous spectrum of photons.

**Key features:**
- **Continuous spectrum** from a minimum wavelength (Duane-Hunt limit) to the applied kVp.
- Energy is proportional to the **square of the atomic number (Z²)** of the target.
- Energy is proportional to the **accelerating voltage (kVp)**.

### 2.1.2 Characteristic Radiation — ~20–30% of clinical X-rays

When an incident electron **ejects an inner-shell (K-shell) electron** of the target atom, an outer-shell (L-shell) electron drops down to fill the vacancy. The energy difference is released as a **discrete, monoenergetic photon** characteristic of the target material.

**Common characteristic lines (Tungsten):**
- **K-α line ≈ 59.3 keV**
- **K-β line ≈ 67.2 keV**

> [!NOTE]
> For characteristic radiation to occur, the incident electron energy must exceed the **K-edge binding energy** of the target (Tungsten K-edge = **69.5 keV**).

## 2.2 X-Ray Interaction with Tissue — The Five Interactions

| # | Interaction | Energy Range | Dominant For | Effect on Image |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Photoelectric Absorption** | 30–80 keV | Bone, Iodine, Barium | High contrast |
| 2 | **Compton (Incoherent) Scattering** | > 80 keV | Soft tissue | Image noise |
| 3 | **Rayleigh (Coherent) Scattering** | < 30 keV | Low-Z tissue | Minor blur |
| 4 | **Pair Production** | > 1.022 MeV | (Not in diagnostic) | Therapy only |
| 5 | **Photodisintegration** | > 7–10 MeV | (Not in diagnostic) | Therapy only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Photoelectric effect** is the **single most important** interaction in diagnostic radiology because its probability scales as **Z³/E³**, providing excellent contrast between bone (high Z) and soft tissue (low Z).

## 2.3 The Beer–Lambert Law of Attenuation

The fundamental governing equation of every X-ray (and CT) image is the **mono-energetic exponential attenuation law**:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = **incident photon intensity** (photons·mm⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $I(x)$ = **transmitted intensity** after traversing thickness $x$
- $\mu$ = **linear attenuation coefficient** (cm⁻¹ or mm⁻¹)
- $x$ = **path length** through tissue (cm or mm)

For a **polychromatic** clinical beam and a body composed of **multiple tissues**, this generalizes to a **line integral** of the attenuation coefficient along ray path $\mathcal{L}$:

$$I = I_0 \exp\left(-\int_{\mathcal{L}} \mu(x, y, z) \, ds\right)$$

This is the **Radon Transform** foundation upon which **Computed Tomography (CT)** is mathematically built.

## 2.4 CT Numbers (Hounsfield Units — HU)

To standardize tissue contrast, Sir **Godfrey Hounsfield** (Nobel Prize 1979) defined a normalized attenuation scale:

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference scale (Standard 120 kVp):**
> - Air = **−1000 HU**
> - Lung parenchyma = **−500 HU**
> - Fat = **−100 HU**
> - Water = **0 HU**
> - CSF = **+15 HU**
> - White matter = **+30 HU**
> - Grey matter = **+40 HU**
> - Blood (clotted) = **+60 to +100 HU**
> - Bone (cortical) = **+700 to +3000 HU**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (|) used — uses `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute values per engine rule.**

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Physical Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Duane–Hunt minimum wavelength | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24}{V(\text{kVp})}\ \text{nm}$ | Shortest emitted photon wavelength | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | X-ray photon energy | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}\ \text{eV}$ | Energy–wavelength duality | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Mono-energetic attenuation | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer–Lambert law | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Mass attenuation coefficient | $\mu_m = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}\ (\text{cm}^2/\text{g})$ | Density-independent contrast | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Photoelectric probability | $P_{pe} \propto \dfrac{Z^3}{E^3}$ | Bone–soft tissue contrast | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Compton probability | $P_c \propto \dfrac{1}{E}$ | High-energy scatter noise | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Hounsfield Unit (CT number) | $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$ | Tissue density scale | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Half-Value Layer (HVL) | $\text{HVL} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | Beam hardening index | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Tube current × time product | $\text{mAs} = I \cdot t$ | Exposure control parameter | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Skin entrance dose (ESD) | $\text{ESD} = k \cdot \text{mAs} \cdot \left(\dfrac{100}{\text{FSD}}\right)^2$ | Patient dose estimation | ⭐⭐ |
| 11 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (CT) | $\text{SNR} \propto \sqrt{\text{mAs}} \cdot \sqrt{N}$ | Image quality metric | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | Quantum detection efficiency | $\text{QDE} = \eta \cdot g$ | Detector sensitivity | ⭐⭐ |

**Standard X-ray constant values:**

- $h$ (Planck's constant) = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ (Speed of light) = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$
- $1\ \text{keV} = 1000\ \text{eV}$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The X-ray system is the **workhorse** of:
- **Emergency trauma imaging** (portable digital radiography in OT/ICU)
- **Cancer detection & staging** (mammography, chest X-ray, CT thorax)
- **Interventional radiology** (fluoroscopy-guided angioplasty)
- **CAD (Computer-Aided Detection)** for tuberculosis and lung nodules
- **AI-driven triage** of chest radiographs in modern PACS workflows
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Duane–Hunt Minimum Wavelength

**Statement:** The shortest wavelength of X-rays emitted from a tube is inversely proportional to the applied peak tube voltage.

**Starting from energy conservation:**

The entire kinetic energy of an incident electron accelerated through potential difference $V$ is converted to **one photon** at minimum wavelength.

$$\begin{aligned}
eV &= E_{\text{photon,max}} \\
eV &= \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}} \\
\lambda_{\min} &= \frac{hc}{eV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Substituting constants** ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C):

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\min} &= \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot V(\text{volts})} \\
\lambda_{\min} &= \frac{1.2407 \times 10^{-6}}{V}\ \text{meters}
\end{aligned}$$

**Converting to nanometers and $V$ in kVp** (multiply $V$ by 1000, multiply $\lambda$ by $10^9$):

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.24}{V(\text{kVp})}\ \text{nanometers}}$$

**Numerical Example:** For a tube operated at **100 kVp**:
$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.24}{100} = 0.0124\ \text{nm} = 12.4\ \text{pm}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Attenuation Coefficient from Beer–Lambert Law

**Statement:** Show that the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ represents the fractional reduction in beam intensity per unit thickness.

**Definition via differential equation:**

Consider an infinitesimally thin tissue slab of thickness $dx$. The fractional intensity loss $dI/I$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dx} &= -\mu \, I \\
\frac{dI}{I} &= -\mu \, dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Integrating from $0$ to $x$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} &= -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx \\
\ln I(x) - \ln I_0 &= -\mu x \\
\ln\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right) &= -\mu x \\
\frac{I(x)}{I_0} &= e^{-\mu x}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}}$$

**Verification via Half-Value Layer (HVL):**
At $x = \text{HVL}$, the intensity is halved: $I = I_0/2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_0}{2} &= I_0 \, e^{-\mu \cdot \text{HVL}} \\
\frac{1}{2} &= e^{-\mu \cdot \text{HVL}} \\
\ln 2 &= \mu \cdot \text{HVL} \\
\text{HVL} &= \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hounsfield Unit for Cortical Bone

**Given:** $\mu_{\text{bone}} = 0.528\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ and $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.192\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ at 73 keV.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HU}_{\text{bone}} &= 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{bone}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}} \\
&= 1000 \times \frac{0.528 - 0.192}{0.192} \\
&= 1000 \times \frac{0.336}{0.192} \\
&= 1000 \times 1.75 \\
\text{HU}_{\text{bone}} &= +1750\ \text{HU}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls within the accepted clinical range of **+700 to +3000 HU** for cortical bone. ✅

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Image Contrast in a Chest X-Ray

**Scenario:** A 5 cm pulmonary nodule with $\mu_n = 0.220\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ embedded in lung parenchyma with $\mu_p = 0.050\ \text{cm}^{-1}$.

**Intensity through normal lung (path = 25 cm):**

$$I_p = I_0 \cdot e^{-0.050 \times 25} = I_0 \cdot e^{-1.25} = 0.2865 \, I_0$$

**Intensity through lesion (nodule replaces 5 cm of lung):**

$$I_n = I_0 \cdot e^{-[0.050 \times 20 + 0.220 \times 5]} = I_0 \cdot e^{-[1.00 + 1.10]} = I_0 \cdot e^{-2.10} = 0.1225 \, I_0$$

**Weber Contrast Formula:**

$$C = \frac{I_p - I_n}{I_p} = \frac{0.2865 - 0.1225}{0.2865} = \frac{0.1640}{0.2865} \approx 0.572\ (\approx 57.2\%)$$

This high contrast is precisely why **chest radiography** remains the **gold standard** for pulmonary nodule detection.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Clinical Application Summary

| Clinical Task | Modality Used | kVp Range | Key Physical Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chest X-ray (PA view) | Digital Radiography | 110–125 kVp | Compton + Photoelectric |
| Mammography | Dedicated Mo/Rh system | 25–35 kVp | Strong photoelectric (Z³) |
| Abdominal X-ray | DR | 70–90 kVp | Differential gut gas contrast |
| CT Head (non-contrast) | MDCT | 120 kVp | HU differentiation |
| CT Pulmonary Angiogram | MDCT + Iodine | 100–120 kVp | K-edge (33.2 keV) of iodine |
| Interventional angio | Fluoroscopy | 60–80 kVp | Real-time projection |
| Dental panoramic | DR | 60–80 kVp | Tomographic reconstruction |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic X-ray radiation. List two clinical applications where each is dominant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Board key pattern):**

| Parameter | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic X-ray |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Deceleration of electrons in nuclear Coulomb field | Inner-shell electron transitions (K→L, L→M) |
| **Spectrum** | Continuous, broad | Discrete, line spectrum (K-α, K-β) |
| **Depends on** | kVp applied | Target element's atomic number |
| **Dominant in** | General radiography, high-kVp chest imaging | Mammography (Mo target), CT contrast studies |
| **Energy range** | 0 to kVp (continuous) | Fixed line energies (e.g., 59.3 keV for W K-α) |

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for applications — full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]`
**Define Hounsfield Unit (HU). What is the HU value of pure water, fat, cortical bone, and air?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** is a normalized linear attenuation coefficient scale, defined as:

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

| Tissue | HU Value |
|---|---|
| **Air** | **−1000 HU** |
| **Fat** | **−100 HU** |
| **Pure Water** | **0 HU** *(reference standard)* |
| **Cortical Bone** | **+700 to +3000 HU** |

*(Mark split: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for the four values — full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Coolidge X-ray tube** with a neatly labeled block diagram. Discuss the role of the cathode, anode, focusing cup, and high-voltage generator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Beer–Lambert attenuation law** and explain how **CT reconstruction** mathematically recovers the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y,z)$ from a series of projection measurements. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Cathode Assembly (2 marks):**
- Consists of a **tungsten filament** (helical coil) heated to **~2400 K** by a low-voltage current (typically 4–6 V, 3–5 A).
- Thermionic emission releases a cloud of electrons (space-charge limited emission — governed by **Richardson's Law**: $J = A_R T^2 e^{-W/kT}$).
- The **focusing cup** (a negatively biased molybdenum shroud) shapes and directs the electron beam onto a small focal spot on the anode (typically 0.6 mm × 0.6 mm for general radiography).

**2. Anode Assembly (2 marks):**
- Made of **tungsten** (Z = 74) due to its high melting point (**3422 °C**), high Z for efficient Bremsstrahlung production, and high thermal conductivity.
- Anode rotates at **3000–10,000 RPM** to distribute heat over a large area (line-focus principle reduces effective focal spot size while allowing high mA).
- The **line-focus principle** gives an effective focal spot of:
$$b = a \cdot \sin \theta$$
where $a$ is the actual focal track width and $\theta$ is the anode angle (typically 7°–20°).

**3. High-Voltage Generator (1 mark):**
- Provides the **accelerating potential (50–150 kVp)** between cathode and anode.
- Modern units use **high-frequency (50–100 kHz) inverter generators** for stable, low-ripple output.
- Output is **rectified and filtered** to produce near-DC, ensuring reproducible beam quality.

**4. Vacuum Envelope and Window (1 mark):**
- The tube is evacuated to **~10⁻⁶ Torr** to prevent electron scattering and filament oxidation.
- X-rays exit through a **beryllium window** (low Z = 4, high transmission, only 0.5 mm thick).

**5. Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.4 (XRayTube assembly).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Beer–Lambert Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the differential form of intensity loss:

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -\mu I$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = x$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}}$$

**Step 2: Generalization to a 3D Body (2 marks):**

For a polychromatic beam traversing a non-homogeneous body along ray path $\mathcal{L}$:

$$I = I_0 \exp\left(-\int_{\mathcal{L}} \mu(x,y,z) \, ds\right)$$

The projection $p$ measured by the detector is the **negative logarithm** of the intensity ratio:

$$p = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_{\mathcal{L}} \mu(x,y,z) \, ds$$

**Step 3: CT Reconstruction (2 marks):**

This integral is precisely the **Radon Transform** $\mathcal{R}\{\mu\}(\theta, s)$. CT reconstruction is the **inverse Radon transform** problem.

The classical method is **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)**:

1. **Acquire** $N$ projections at angles $\theta_i$, $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ (typically $N \geq 720$ for clinical CT).
2. **Filter** each projection with a high-pass kernel (Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan, or Cosine filter) in the Fourier domain to compensate for the **$1/\vert f \vert$ blurring** inherent in simple back-projection.
3. **Back-project** the filtered projections across the image matrix.
4. **Modern iterative methods** (ASIR, SAFIRE, ADMIRE, model-based IR) use statistical cost functions and regularization to suppress noise at low doses.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **five primary interactions of X-rays with matter**. Which interaction is the most important for **diagnostic radiology** and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An X-ray tube is operated at **80 kVp** and **200 mAs**. Calculate the **minimum wavelength** of the emitted X-ray photons and the **expected Hounsfield Unit** of a tissue sample with $\mu_t = 0.30\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ at 73 keV. Given $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.192\ \text{cm}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Five Interactions (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Interaction | Energy Range | Mechanism | Diagnostic Importance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Photoelectric Absorption** | 30–80 keV | Photon absorbed, inner-shell electron ejected | **Most important** — provides image contrast (Z³/E³) |
| 2 | **Compton Scattering** | > 80 keV | Photon scatters off outer-shell electron | Major noise/degraded contrast source |
| 3 | **Rayleigh (Coherent) Scattering** | < 30 keV | Photon scatters without energy loss | Minor blur, low energy |
| 4 | **Pair Production** | > 1.022 MeV | Photon → electron-positron pair | **Not used diagnostically** (therapy only) |
| 5 | **Photodisintegration** | > 7–10 MeV | Photon absorbed by nucleus | **Not used diagnostically** (therapy only) |

**Why Photoelectric is Most Important (2 marks):**
- Probability scales as $\mathbf{Z^3/E^3}$, providing **excellent contrast between bone (Z_eff ≈ 13.8) and soft tissue (Z_eff ≈ 7.4)**.
- Strongly dependent on the **K-edge** of contrast agents (e.g., Iodine K-edge = 33.2 keV, Barium K-edge = 37.4 keV) — exploited in **angiography** and **barium studies**.
- Maximized in the **diagnostic energy range (40–80 keV)**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Minimum Wavelength (3 marks):**

Using the **Duane–Hunt relation**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\min} &= \frac{1.24}{V(\text{kVp})}\ \text{nm} \\
\lambda_{\min} &= \frac{1.24}{80} \\
\lambda_{\min} &= 0.0155\ \text{nm} = 15.5\ \text{pm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent Photon Energy (1 mark — bonus step):**

$$E_{\max} = eV = 80\ \text{keV}$$

Verification:
$$E = \frac{1.24}{0.0155} = 80\ \text{keV} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Hounsfield Unit Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HU} &= 1000 \times \frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w} \\
\text{HU} &= 1000 \times \frac{0.30 - 0.192}{0.192} \\
\text{HU} &= 1000 \times \frac{0.108}{0.192} \\
\text{HU} &= 1000 \times 0.5625 \\
\boxed{\text{HU}} &= +562.5\ \text{HU}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Clinical Interpretation (1 mark):**
A value of **+562.5 HU** falls within the range of **soft tissue with calcification** or **early bone formation / hyperdense material**, between normal soft tissue (+30 to +60 HU) and cortical bone (+700 to +3000 HU).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–4 marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Writing $|x|$ in markdown tables** — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX to avoid broken table rendering.
> 2. **Forgetting the kVp unit** in the Duane–Hunt formula — examiners deduct 1 mark if $\lambda_{\min} = 1.24/V$ is written without the **(kVp)** qualifier.
> 3. **Confusing HU of fat vs. water** — Water is **0 HU** (reference); fat is **−100 HU** (NOT −1000).
> 4. **Mixing up HVL formula** — the correct form is $\text{HVL} = \ln 2 \, / \, \mu$, NOT $1/\mu$.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the K-edge** of contrast media — Iodine's 33.2 keV K-edge is a **guaranteed 1-mark question** in KTU ESE.
> 6. **Skipping the unit in the final answer** — "HU = +562.5" loses 0.5 marks; "HU = +562.5 HU" gets full credit.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ X-rays were **discovered by Röntgen in 1895** and are the **first medical imaging modality**.
- ✅ Clinical X-ray energy range: **50–150 kVp**; minimum wavelength given by **$\lambda_{\min} = 1.24 / V(\text{kVp})$ nm**.
- ✅ X-ray spectrum has **two components**: **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous) and **Characteristic** (discrete lines).
- ✅ The **Photoelectric effect** is the dominant diagnostic interaction: $P \propto Z^3/E^3$.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ is the foundation of radiography and CT.
- ✅ **Hounsfield Unit:** $\text{HU} = 1000 \times (\mu_t - \mu_w) / \mu_w$ — Air = **−1000**, Water = **0**, Bone = **+700 to +3000**.
- ✅ The **CT reconstruction problem** is mathematically the **Inverse Radon Transform**, solved by **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** or **iterative reconstruction (IR)**.
- ✅ **Iodine K-edge = 33.2 keV** and **Barium K-edge = 37.4 keV** — exploited in angiography and GI contrast studies.
- ✅ **HVL (Half-Value Layer)** = $\ln 2 / \mu$ — quantifies beam hardening and required filtration.
- ✅ Always quote the **kVp unit** in the Duane–Hunt formula and the **HU unit** in CT number answers — KTU examiners are strict.
- ✅ Anode is **tungsten (Z = 74)**; the cathode filament is heated to **~2400 K** for thermionic emission.
- ✅ A chest X-ray delivers ~**0.02 mSv**; a CT thorax delivers ~**7 mSv** — always reference **ALARA** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principle.
- ✅ Modern digital systems use **CsI:Tl scintillators** coupled to **amorphous-Si TFT arrays** (Flat Panel Detectors).
- ✅ The **line-focus principle** enables a small effective focal spot while distributing heat over a large anode track area.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme — this topic maps directly to **CO2 (Understand)** and **CO4 (Analyze)** of PEBMT754.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
